_meta.description: Data Pilots Trust Check — canonical checks registry. The 5x5 framework: 5 focus areas × 5 maturity levels. Product tier is a separate commercial dimension.

transparency.label: Transparency
transparency.description: Evaluates how clearly and openly you communicate your data collection, usage, and sharing practices to individuals.
transparency.examples: Communication methods, privacy notices, individual participation (consent, opt-in/opt-out, preferences).

data_storage_security.label: Data Storage & Security
data_storage_security.description: Evaluates your methods for securely storing data and protecting it from unauthorised access and breaches.
data_storage_security.examples: Data storage management, access controls, security measures, data retention and destruction.

data_sharing_transfers.label: Data Sharing & Transfers
data_sharing_transfers.description: Examines how you manage and safeguard the sharing and transfer of data, especially across borders, to align with legal and regulatory requirements.
data_sharing_transfers.examples: Third-party due diligence, security measures, risk management, awareness of international requirements.

governance_data_usage.label: Governance & Data Usage
governance_data_usage.description: Examines your policies, procedures, and practices for managing data responsibly.
governance_data_usage.examples: Incident response plan, restricting use of personal information, regular review of business processes, reviews and audits, awareness and staying updated.

data_rights_control.label: Data Rights & Control
data_rights_control.description: Assesses how well you empower individuals to exercise their data rights, including access, correct, delete, and control the use of their personal data.
data_rights_control.examples: Inform individuals of rights, process to submit data requests, technical measures to modify use, data requests management.

1.label: Privacy-Aware
1.description: Your organisation is at the beginning stages of developing data privacy practices. Basic awareness of data privacy issues exists, but formal processes and/or policies are not yet established.

2.label: Privacy-Proficient
2.description: Your organisation has implemented basic data privacy practices. There are soon established processes, however these may not be consistently applied or regularly reviewed.

3.label: Privacy-Savvy
3.description: Your organisation has a well-designed data privacy framework. Privacy practices are documented, communicated, and integrated into operations.

4.label: Privacy-Driven
4.description: Your organisation uses metrics and data to manage and improve data privacy practices. There is a systematic approach to measuring performance and compliance but is capable of further improvement.

5.label: Privacy-Advanced
5.description: Your organisation has achieved the highest level of data privacy maturity. Data privacy practices are well-established and are continuously improved (for example through feedback loops and advanced technologies).

product_tiers.free: Public website scan. No account required. Surfaces visible signals — what a buyer or auditor can see without asking.
product_tiers.paid: Deeper scan within each framework area. Account required. Results saved and trackable over time.
product_tiers.paid_plus: Operational and organisational checks. Portal/platform scan. Human-in-the-loop guidance and remediation support.

trust_pillars.transparency: Do you clearly tell buyers and users what you do with their data?
trust_pillars.safety: Is your site technically secure and protected against known vulnerabilities?
trust_pillars.ux: Can buyers find what they need to feel confident before saying yes?
trust_pillars.ai_trust: Are you transparent about how you use AI — for buyers and for learners?

maturity_to_tier_guide.note: Maturity level describes the organisation's state. Product tier describes what the scanner checks. These are related but not the same.
maturity_to_tier_guide.free: Primarily surfaces level 1–2 signals — what's visible on the public website.
maturity_to_tier_guide.paid: Assesses level 2–3 depth — are things documented, consistent, and properly implemented?
maturity_to_tier_guide.paid_plus: Assesses level 3–4 depth — are things integrated, measured, and regularly reviewed?

checks.name: HTTPS / Secure Connection
checks.what_we_check: Whether the site loads over HTTPS
checks.why_it_matters_business: Browsers mark HTTP sites as 'Not Secure'. Any buyer who sees that label has immediate doubt before the conversation even starts.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: Encryption in transit required under GDPR Art. 32. Baseline for ISO 27001, Cyber Essentials, and SOC 2 CC6.
checks.fix_guide: Contact your hosting provider and enable an SSL certificate. Most modern hosts (Squarespace, Wix, WordPress) do this for free in one click.

checks.name: HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security)
checks.what_we_check: Whether the Strict-Transport-Security response header is present
checks.why_it_matters_business: HSTS tells browsers to always use HTTPS. Without it, a sophisticated attacker can intercept the first connection.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 32 technical safeguards. Aligns with Cyber Essentials and NIST PR.DS (protect data in transit).
checks.fix_guide: Add `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains` to your server or CDN headers.

checks.name: Content Security Policy (CSP)
checks.what_we_check: Whether a Content-Security-Policy header is set
checks.why_it_matters_business: CSP prevents malicious script injection. Procurement security questionnaires explicitly ask about this.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 32 technical safeguards. ISO 27001 A.8 (application security). SOC 2 CC6.
checks.fix_guide: Start with a basic policy via your CDN (Cloudflare, Netlify) or hosting headers. A developer can set a starter policy in under an hour.
follow_up_questions_paid: Do you have a developer who manages your hosting and server config?
Are you on a CDN like Cloudflare that allows header rules without code changes?

checks.name: Clickjacking Protection
checks.what_we_check: X-Frame-Options or CSP frame-ancestors directive
checks.why_it_matters_business: Prevents your site being embedded invisibly inside a malicious page to trick users.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: ISO 27001 A.8 (application security). Cyber Essentials.
checks.fix_guide: Add `X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN` header, or use `frame-ancestors 'self'` in your CSP.

checks.name: Privacy Policy Page
checks.what_we_check: Whether a privacy policy page exists at a standard path
checks.why_it_matters_business: 73% of L&D buyers check for a privacy policy before signing. Missing = immediate red flag in procurement.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: Required under GDPR Art. 13/14, UK DPA 2018, and CCPA §1798.100.
checks.fix_guide: Create a /privacy-policy page. Use a GDPR-compliant generator (e.g. iubenda, Termly) as a starting point then customise to reflect how you actually use data.
follow_up_questions_paid: Have you had your privacy policy reviewed by a solicitor or data protection advisor?
Is your privacy policy the same for website visitors and for your learners/clients?
When was your privacy policy last reviewed or updated?

checks.name: GDPR Language in Privacy Policy
checks.what_we_check: Whether the privacy policy includes GDPR concepts: lawful basis, data subject rights, data controller, supervisory authority
checks.why_it_matters_business: UK and EU enterprise procurement teams will reject suppliers whose privacy policy doesn't reference GDPR.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 13/14 requires specific disclosures including lawful basis, data subject rights, and supervisory authority.
checks.fix_guide: Update your policy to name: lawful basis for processing, data subject rights (access, erasure, portability, objection), and the ICO as supervisory authority (UK).
follow_up_questions_paid: Have you registered with the ICO (UK) as a data controller?
Do you have a named Data Protection Officer or responsible person?

checks.name: Privacy Policy — Required Topics
checks.what_we_check: Whether the privacy policy covers: what is collected, why, third-party sharing, visitor rights, and contact details
checks.why_it_matters_business: A policy missing any of these is unlikely to satisfy enterprise procurement requirements.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 13 and 14 require all five topics for lawful processing.
checks.fix_guide: Review your policy against each topic and add a section for any that are missing. Plain language works better than legal boilerplate for both buyers and regulators.

checks.name: Privacy Policy — Last Updated Date
checks.what_we_check: Whether the privacy policy shows a visible 'last updated' or 'effective date'
checks.why_it_matters_business: Policies without a date look stale. It signals data governance hasn't been reviewed recently.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR accountability principle (Art. 5(2)). ISO 27001 document control. Good practice under ICO guidance.
checks.fix_guide: Add 'Last updated: [date]' to the top of your privacy policy. Review and re-date at least annually.

checks.name: CCPA / US Privacy Language
checks.what_we_check: Whether the privacy policy addresses CCPA rights: do not sell, right to know, right to delete
checks.why_it_matters_business: If you have US-based clients (especially California), CCPA compliance is legally required and increasingly expected in enterprise procurement.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: California Consumer Privacy Act §1798.100 et seq.
checks.fix_guide: Add a CCPA section to your privacy policy covering right to know, right to delete, and opt-out of sale.
follow_up_questions_paid: Do you have clients or learners based in California or other US states?
Do you sell or share personal data with third parties for advertising purposes?

checks.name: Data Processing Agreement Language
checks.what_we_check: Whether the privacy policy or site references Data Processing Agreements, sub-processors, or standard contractual clauses
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise and public sector clients almost always require a signed DPA before a contract is issued.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 28 requires a written DPA between controller and processor. SCCs required for international transfers.
checks.fix_guide: Draft a standard DPA template you can offer to enterprise clients. List your sub-processors. A data consultant or solicitor can help draft this in a day.
follow_up_questions_paid: Have you ever been asked to sign a Data Processing Agreement by a client?
Do you have a standard DPA template you can share with clients?
What tools do you use that process learner or client personal data?
Are any of your tools US-based? (relevant for international data transfer rules)

checks.name: Terms of Service / Use Page
checks.what_we_check: Whether a terms of service or terms and conditions page exists
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise procurement requires clear terms before signing. A missing terms page can stall a deal at the contract stage.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: Provides legal clarity on data responsibilities and liability. Referenced in GDPR Art. 28 controller-processor relationships.
checks.fix_guide: Create a /terms page covering: what the service includes, payment terms, IP ownership, liability limits, and cancellation policy.
follow_up_questions_paid: Do you have standard terms you send clients, or do you negotiate bespoke contracts?
Do your terms reference GDPR obligations or data handling?

checks.name: Dedicated Cookie Policy / Disclosure Page
checks.what_we_check: Whether a dedicated cookie policy or cookie disclosure page exists
checks.why_it_matters_business: Many privacy-aware buyers check for this specifically. A missing cookie disclosure is one of the first things flagged in a privacy audit.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR and UK PECR require clear disclosure of cookies: purpose, duration, and how to opt out.
checks.fix_guide: Create a /cookie-policy page listing each cookie category, specific cookies, purpose, duration, and opt-out instructions.

checks.name: Policy Links in Footer
checks.what_we_check: Whether Privacy Policy and Terms links are visible in the site footer
checks.why_it_matters_business: Buyers look in the footer first. Not finding policy links there creates doubt before they've read anything.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR requires privacy information to be 'easily accessible'. Footer placement is the recognised standard.
checks.fix_guide: Add Privacy Policy and Terms of Service links to your footer. They should be visible on every page.

checks.name: Cookie Policy Link in Footer
checks.what_we_check: Whether a cookie policy link is visible in the footer
checks.why_it_matters_business: Privacy-aware buyers and regulators expect cookie disclosures to be easy to find.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: UK PECR and GDPR require cookie information to be accessible. Footer is the standard location.
checks.fix_guide: Add a 'Cookie Policy' link to your footer alongside your Privacy Policy.

checks.name: Contact Page
checks.what_we_check: Whether a contact or about page exists
checks.why_it_matters_business: If buyers can't find how to contact you, they lose confidence. Visible contact details are a basic trust signal.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 12 requires a way for data subjects to reach the data controller to exercise their rights.
checks.fix_guide: Ensure you have a /contact or /about page with a working email, phone, or contact form. A physical address adds credibility for enterprise buyers.
follow_up_questions_paid: Do you include your registered business address on your site?
Do you have a named individual (not just info@) as a point of contact for data queries?

checks.name: Cookie Consent Language (HTML scan)
checks.what_we_check: Whether cookie, GDPR, consent, or data protection language appears in the page HTML
checks.why_it_matters_business: No consent language is often a sign there is no consent mechanism at all — one of the most common trust failures we find.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR and UK PECR require freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous consent before non-essential cookies fire.
checks.fix_guide: Implement a consent management platform (CMP) such as Cookiebot, CookieYes, or OneTrust.

checks.name: Consent Banner — Live Browser Check
checks.what_we_check: Whether a real browser agent visiting your site sees a consent banner with Accept, Decline, and Settings options
checks.why_it_matters_business: An HTML scan can miss a banner loaded via JavaScript. Papaya's agent sees exactly what a real buyer sees.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR and UK PECR require a clear, accessible mechanism for consent and withdrawal. ICO has fined organisations for non-functional consent banners.
checks.fix_guide: Implement a CMP. Ensure the banner appears on first load before any non-essential cookies fire.

checks.name: Reject-All Effectiveness — Live Browser Check
checks.what_we_check: Whether clicking 'Decline All' actually stops tracking — comparing tracker counts before and after
checks.why_it_matters_business: Many sites have a Decline All button that doesn't work. This is one of the most common GDPR enforcement findings.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 7(3) requires withdrawal of consent to be as easy as giving it. A non-functional reject button is a direct violation. ICO and EU DPAs have issued significant fines.
checks.fix_guide: Work with your CMP provider to ensure Decline All actually blocks cookie firing — not just hides the banner.

checks.name: Pre-Consent Tracking Volume — Live Browser Check
checks.what_we_check: Number of trackers firing on page load before any consent is given
checks.why_it_matters_business: High pre-consent tracker counts are a direct compliance risk and surface immediately in a procurement privacy audit.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 6 requires consent before non-essential processing begins. Pre-consent tracking is prima facie evidence of unlawful processing.
checks.fix_guide: Configure your CMP to block all non-essential scripts from loading until consent is given.

checks.name: Consent Management Platform Identified
checks.what_we_check: Whether Papaya identifies a recognised CMP (OneTrust, Cookiebot, etc.)
checks.why_it_matters_business: Using a recognised CMP signals mature consent governance. Enterprise procurement teams often verify this.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: ICO guidance recommends using a recognised CMP. Required for IAB TCF compliance.
checks.fix_guide: Consider Cookiebot, CookieYes, or OneTrust depending on your budget and scale.
follow_up_questions_paid: Which consent management platform are you currently using?
Does your CMP log consent records so you can evidence compliance if audited?

checks.name: Consent Category Granularity
checks.what_we_check: Whether the consent banner offers granular categories (analytics, marketing, functional) vs a single accept/reject
checks.why_it_matters_business: A blanket accept/reject without categories signals a less mature consent setup to procurement teams.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR requires specific consent per purpose (Art. 6). A blanket consent doesn't meet the 'specific' requirement. ICO enforcement has focused on this.
checks.fix_guide: Configure your CMP to offer separate consent toggles for at least: Strictly Necessary, Analytics/Performance, and Marketing/Advertising.

checks.name: AI Tool Disclosure (Public Site)
checks.what_we_check: Whether the site mentions AI tools, partners, or AI-powered features
checks.why_it_matters_business: Over half of enterprise L&D buyers now ask about AI use before signing. A proactive disclosure prevents it becoming a procurement blocker.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 22 requires disclosure of automated decision-making. EU AI Act (2026+) requires transparency for high-risk AI in education and employment.
checks.fix_guide: Add a short plain-language note on your website describing how and where you use AI — and what data is involved.
follow_up_questions_paid: Which AI tools do you use in your L&D delivery or content creation?
Does any AI tool you use process learner data (names, scores, chat history)?
Do your client contracts or terms mention AI use?
Have any clients asked you to complete an AI risk assessment or questionnaire?

checks.name: Sitemap
checks.what_we_check: Whether a sitemap.xml exists
checks.why_it_matters_business: A sitemap is a minor signal of a well-maintained, professionally run site.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: No direct compliance requirement. Supports accessibility and discoverability.
checks.fix_guide: Most website builders generate sitemaps automatically. Check your platform settings.

checks.name: Learner Data Notice in LMS / Portal
checks.what_we_check: Whether your learning portal or platform shows learners a data notice at point of collection
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise HR and legal teams will ask: 'What are your learners told about how their data is used?' You need a clear answer.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 13 requires information to be provided at the time personal data are obtained. Learner completion records and activity logs are personal data.
checks.fix_guide: Add a learner-facing data notice to your LMS registration or onboarding. Explain what's collected, why, how long it's kept, and how learners can request access or deletion.
follow_up_questions_paid: Which LMS or learning platform do you use?
What learner data do you collect? (completion, quiz scores, time-on-task, etc.)
Who can access individual learner data — your team, the learner, or the client employer?
How long do you retain learner completion and activity records?

checks.name: Learner Data Retention Policy
checks.what_we_check: Whether you have a documented retention period for learner data
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise clients will ask 'how long do you keep our employees' data?' — you need a clear, documented answer.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR storage limitation principle (Art. 5(1)(e)). Must be documented under accountability principle (Art. 5(2)).
checks.fix_guide: Define and document retention periods (e.g. completion records kept 3 years then deleted or anonymised). Include in privacy policy and DPA.
follow_up_questions_paid: How long do you currently keep learner completion and activity data?
Is there a process to delete or anonymise data on request?
Do you have a data deletion schedule, or is it ad hoc?

checks.name: Third-Party Tools in Learning Delivery
checks.what_we_check: What third-party tools process learner data (video platforms, LMS, email tools, analytics)
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise clients will ask for this list during procurement. Not having it ready signals immature data governance.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 28 requires sub-processors to be disclosed and contracted. Clients have the right to object to sub-processor changes.
checks.fix_guide: List every tool that processes learner or client personal data. Ensure each has a DPA with you. List them as sub-processors in your privacy policy.
follow_up_questions_paid: Which video conferencing tool do you use for live learning?
Which LMS or platform hosts your content?
Do you use any AI-powered tools that learners interact with directly?
Do you use email marketing tools to communicate with learners or clients?

checks.name: AI Disclosure to Learners
checks.what_we_check: Whether learners are told when AI is involved in their learning experience
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise clients are increasingly asking about this — and regulation is catching up fast in education and employment contexts.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 22 (automated decision-making). EU AI Act provisions for high-risk AI in education/employment. Emerging ICO guidance on AI transparency.
checks.fix_guide: Add an AI disclosure to your learner onboarding. Be specific about what AI does in their experience and whether it affects assessment or personalisation.
follow_up_questions_paid: Does your platform use AI for personalisation, recommendation, or automated feedback?
Are learners ever assessed by an AI system (not a human)?
Is AI-generated content used in any of your courses? Is this disclosed?

checks.name: Data Breach / Incident Response Plan
checks.what_we_check: Whether the business has a documented process for responding to a data breach or security incident
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise clients in regulated sectors will ask 'what happens if there's a breach?' during procurement. Not having a process is a red flag.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 33/34 require breach notification to the ICO within 72 hours and to affected individuals without undue delay. Requires a plan to be in place before an incident.
checks.fix_guide: Document a simple incident response plan: who to notify, how to contain, how to report to the ICO, and how to communicate to affected individuals. One page is enough to start.
follow_up_questions_paid: Have you ever experienced a data breach or security incident?
Do you know your obligation to notify the ICO within 72 hours of a breach?
Is there a named person responsible for data breach response in your business?

checks.name: Regular Privacy Review Process
checks.what_we_check: Whether the business has a documented process for regular privacy reviews (at least annually)
checks.why_it_matters_business: Privacy-Driven organisations review and improve their practices systematically. This is what separates businesses that can evidence compliance from those that just have a document.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR accountability principle (Art. 5(2)) requires demonstrable compliance. ISO 27001 requires management reviews. Regular reviews are explicitly expected by ICO guidance.
checks.fix_guide: Schedule an annual privacy review: check policies are up to date, review what data you hold, verify sub-processors, and update your record of processing activities (RoPA).
follow_up_questions_paid: When did you last formally review your privacy policies and practices?
Do you maintain a Record of Processing Activities (RoPA)?
Have you carried out a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) for any high-risk processing?

checks.name: Internal AI Governance Policy
checks.what_we_check: Whether the business has a documented policy for responsible AI use
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise clients in regulated sectors now ask suppliers for evidence of AI governance. A one-page AI policy is often enough to pass initial procurement screening.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: EU AI Act requires governance for high-risk AI. ICO AI guidance recommends documented accountability. ISO 42001 (AI Management System) is an emerging standard.
checks.fix_guide: Draft a short AI use policy covering: which tools you use, what data they access, how outputs are quality-checked, and who is responsible for AI decisions.
follow_up_questions_paid: Does your team have formal guidance on which AI tools are approved for use?
Have any enterprise clients asked you to complete an AI risk assessment?

checks.name: Standard DPA Available for Clients
checks.what_we_check: Whether the business has a signed or signable DPA template ready for enterprise clients
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise and public sector clients almost universally require a signed DPA before awarding a contract.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 28 — mandatory written agreement between controller and processor. Absence = non-compliant processing.
checks.fix_guide: Work with a data protection solicitor to create a standard DPA naming you as data processor, listing sub-processors, defining retention periods, and referencing your security measures.
follow_up_questions_paid: Have you signed a DPA with any of your current clients?
Do you have a solicitor or data protection advisor you work with?
Are any of your clients public sector or regulated industry (NHS, financial services, education)?

checks.name: Sub-Processor List Published
checks.what_we_check: Whether the business publishes or can provide a list of sub-processors
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise clients will ask for this list during procurement. Not having it ready signals immature data governance.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 28(3)(d) — processor must provide controller with information about sub-processors. Controllers have right to object to new sub-processors.
checks.fix_guide: Create a sub-processor list with: tool name, purpose, data location, link to their privacy/security page. Keep it updated.
follow_up_questions_paid: Can you list the key tools your business uses that handle client or learner data?
Are any of these tools based outside the UK/EU? (affects international transfer rules)

checks.name: Privacy Programme Continuous Improvement
checks.what_we_check: Whether the business has feedback loops, metrics, and a systematic process for continuously improving privacy practices
checks.why_it_matters_business: Privacy-Advanced organisations can demonstrate ongoing improvement — not just a one-time compliance exercise. This is increasingly what top-tier enterprise clients expect.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: ISO 27001 continual improvement principle. GDPR accountability (Art. 5(2)) requires demonstrable, ongoing compliance — not just a snapshot.
checks.fix_guide: Establish a privacy metrics dashboard: track consent rates, data subject requests, breach incidents, policy review dates, and sub-processor changes. Review quarterly.
follow_up_questions_paid: Do you track any privacy metrics (consent rates, data requests, incidents)?
Do you have a privacy champion or DPO who owns continuous improvement?
Have you achieved or are you working toward any privacy certifications (ISO 27001, Cyber Essentials, etc.)?
